planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Canada anemone spreads vigorously by rhizomes and can be aggressive in small gardens — use it in naturalized areas, rain gardens, and large native plantings where spreading is desired, or confine with a root barrier.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Canada anemone spreads vigorously by rhizomes and can be aggressive in small gardens — use it in naturalized areas, rain gardens, and large native plantings where spreading is desired, or confine with a root barrier. Anemone canadensis produces dense, attractive clumps of deeply lobed, sharply toothed, dark-green leaves and a long succession of cheerful, bright-white, five-petaled flowers with golden-yellow centers from late spring through summer; it is one of the most prolific and reliable white-flowering perennials for moist sites and looks beautiful mass-planted along stream banks, pond margins, and the edges of open woodlands. The flowers are among the most important late-spring native bee resources in the central and northeastern US.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, average to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; thimbleweed is more drought-tolerant than Canada anemone and prefers better-drained, drier soils in open woodland or prairie edge settings.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, average to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; thimbleweed is more drought-tolerant than Canada anemone and prefers better-drained, drier soils in open woodland or prairie edge settings. Anemone virginiana is a compact, clump-forming native with deeply dissected leaves, and produces greenish-white, sometimes pink-tinged flowers from June through August, followed by its most distinctive and ornamental feature: cylindrical, spool-shaped seed heads covered in cottony white seeds that persist well into winter and provide excellent winter interest; the fluffy seed heads catch the winter light and are outstanding when used in dried arrangements. It naturalizes modestly from self-seed without becoming invasive and is well-suited to dry shade gardens, woodland margins, and native butterfly garden plantings.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; columbine meadow rue will tolerate full sun if soil remains consistently moist but looks most refined in the dappled shade of an open woodland garden.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; columbine meadow rue will tolerate full sun if soil remains consistently moist but looks most refined in the dappled shade of an open woodland garden. Thalictrum aquilegifolium is arguably the most beautiful of the meadow rues, with large, glaucous, blue-green leaves so similar in shape to columbine (Aquilegia) that the plant appears to be a columbine when not in flower; in late spring and early summer it produces large, fluffy, globe-shaped panicles of tiny flowers in lilac-purple, rose, or white (var. album) formed entirely of prominent stamens without petals, giving the flower heads an unusually light, powder-puff texture. It makes an outstanding companion for hostas, ferns, and other shade perennials and is long-lived and essentially care-free once established.

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; tall meadow rue is native to wet meadows, stream banks, and wet woods and demands consistent moisture to thrive — it is ideal for rain gardens, wet meadow borders, and streamside plantings.

directions: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; tall meadow rue is native to wet meadows, stream banks, and wet woods and demands consistent moisture to thrive — it is ideal for rain gardens, wet meadow borders, and streamside plantings. Thalictrum polygamum is the tallest native North American meadow rue, a dramatic architectural plant reaching 5–7 feet in good conditions with large, compound, blue-green leaves and enormous, cloud-like panicles of tiny, creamy-white, fragrant flowers with prominent white stamens in midsummer; it is one of the finest plants for providing vertical scale in large native rain gardens and streamside plantings and provides exceptional late-spring and summer nectar for native bees and beneficial wasps.

planting.note: Start transplants from stratified seed indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost, or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; wild quinine develops a deep taproot after the first year and becomes highly drought-tolerant.

directions: Start transplants from stratified seed indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost, or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; wild quinine develops a deep taproot after the first year and becomes highly drought-tolerant. Parthenium integrifolium is a tough, adaptable native perennial producing bold clusters of flat-topped, button-like white flower heads from late spring through summer on sturdy, upright, 2–4 foot stems with large, textured, rough-hairy leaves; it was used medicinally by Native Americans as a quinine substitute (hence the common name) for fever treatment. The flat flower clusters are outstanding for butterflies, small native bees, and wasps; the plant is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once deep-rooted and thrives in clay or loamy soils in full sun with no supplemental irrigation, making it an outstanding low-maintenance prairie garden plant.
